summary: This paper addresses federated learning in the presence of noisy labels (F-LN), a setting complicated by dual heterogeneity across clients: heterogeneous label-noise types and ratios as well as non-iid data distributions. The starting point is an empirical observation that the FL global model memorizes noisy labels more slowly than a centrally trained model, so it retains comparatively reliable predictions and representations during training. Building on this property, the authors propose Federated Global Reviser (FedGR), which uses the global model as a self-contained denoising and regularization signal through three components: (i) Federated Sieving, where the server fits a Gaussian mixture over per-example loss proxies uploaded by clients to separate likely-clean from likely-noisy samples, combined with client-side Label Refining that corrects noisy labels via global-model-based confidence-weighted pseudo-labeling; (ii) a globally revised EMA distillation, in which each client's EMA teacher is blended toward the global model and distilled into the local model; and (iii) a global representation regularization that aligns local features with the global model's features under weak/strong augmentations. The method is evaluated on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Clothing1M under iid and Dirichlet non-iid partitions, several noise types (symmetric, asymmetric, mixed), and varying proportions of noisy clients, where it consistently outperforms eight state-of-the-art baselines, with the largest gains in the harder high-noise and non-iid regimes. A convergence analysis is also provided.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper studies a genuinely practical and underexplored problem. Label noise is pervasive in real federated deployments, and the joint treatment of label-noise heterogeneity and non-iid data—where standard centralized noisy-label assumptions such as class balance and homogeneous noise break down—is well-motivated and timely. The central observation that the aggregated global model overfits noisy labels more slowly than centralized training is intriguing and, if it holds broadly, offers a reusable insight for the community: it suggests that the global model itself can serve as a better denoising signal than any individual client model. The proposed framework follows coherently from this idea—use the global model to sieve, relabel, and regularize local training—and is easy to follow. The empirical evaluation is a clear strength: it spans multiple datasets, multiple noise regimes including mixed noise, and both iid and Dirichlet partitions, and the gains over strong baselines are consistent and most pronounced in the difficult settings that matter most. The paper is, on the whole, well organized and clearly written, and the inclusion of a convergence analysis is welcome.

My main reservation concerns the soundness of the link between the motivating phenomenon and the method. Figure 1 largely establishes that FL learns—and therefore overfits noise—more slowly than centralized training, but this is also consistent with the global model simply being a delayed or underfit learner rather than possessing an intrinsic noise-resistant property. It is not clear whether the effect is a genuine property of aggregation or a consequence of specific configuration choices such as the number of local epochs, the client sampling ratio, the aggregation rule, or the overall optimization budget; under more aggressive learning rates or substantially longer training the advantage could shrink or disappear. Because the pipeline leans heavily on this property for server-side sieving and label refining, a foundational intuition or at least a preliminary analysis of why aggregation slows noise memorization would considerably strengthen the work. Without it, the three components read somewhat as a well-engineered combination of existing techniques whose individual design rationale is not fully pinned to the observation. Relatedly, although a convergence guarantee is claimed, the supporting assumptions and proof are not surfaced in the main text, which makes the correctness and scope of the result difficult to assess.

The practicality of the method also deserves more scrutiny than it currently receives. Uploading instance-level loss proxies from every client at every round can introduce non-trivial communication cost, particularly at scale, yet this overhead is asserted to be moderate rather than quantified against the standard FL model up/download; the server-side GMM fitting cost as the number of clients and samples grows is likewise not characterized. On the client side, the per-round budget is heavy—multiple forward and backward passes plus maintenance of an EMA model—which could be a real bottleneck on resource-constrained edge devices. The method further introduces several thresholds and balancing weights, and a few of them (e.g., the noise-ratio threshold, the warm-up parameter, and the reliable-label proportion) are not analyzed, leaving it unclear how sensitive FedGR is and how these would be tuned across datasets. There are also robustness questions: how errors propagate when the GMM mis-estimates the noise ratio (e.g., under class imbalance in non-iid partitions), and how the server-side sieving and the global EMA behave under adversarial or heavily noisy clients that could distort the uploaded proxies or temporarily corrupt the global model. Finally, while Clothing1M provides real-world noise, the paper itself notes it is a less faithful proxy for realistic F-LN, so the real-world evidence remains somewhat limited. A number of minor presentation issues (typos, an inconsistency in the set notation, and a missing impact statement) do not affect the substance but should be corrected.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Can you provide intuition or analysis for why the FL global model resists noise memorization beyond simply learning more slowly, and does this phenomenon persist across different aggregation rules, client participation rates, numbers of local epochs, learning rates, prolonged training, and larger-scale datasets?
2. What is the quantitative communication overhead of uploading instance-level loss proxies each round relative to the standard FL model up/download, how does the server-side GMM fitting scale with the number of clients and samples, and can compression or sampling reduce this cost?
3. Could you present the convergence analysis—its assumptions and a proof sketch—within the manuscript so that its correctness and applicability can be judged?
4. How sensitive is FedGR to its thresholds and balancing weights (e.g., the noise-ratio threshold, warm-up, reliable-label proportion, and the regularization/EMA weights), how are they tuned across datasets, and how do errors propagate when the GMM mis-estimates the noise ratio under non-iid class imbalance?
5. How robust is FedGR to malicious clients that manipulate their uploaded loss proxies, or to a global model temporarily poisoned by a cluster of highly noisy clients—does the EMA momentum sufficiently isolate the local models in that case?
limitations: Only partially. The paper mostly defers a theoretical explanation of the memorization phenomenon to future work and under-reports the communication and client-side computational costs as well as the sensitivity to its several hyperparameters. These practical constraints, together with the reliance on an as-yet-unexplained robustness property, should be stated explicitly as limitations so that readers can gauge when the method is expected to work.
